speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 5, the interview with Tola Hauptman. Tola, you were talking about getting your papers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --getting your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so I got my [INAUDIBLE], and I went back where I was. Of course, the Germans usually didn't recognize the Jews by small sort of characteristics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask before you go on  with that, what was the name on your card?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yrena ?] [? Tishinska. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was off that girl. It was bought, of course, the [INAUDIBLE], and then changed with the-- my photograph and my signature. But one thing which wasn't there-- because you have to be [POLISH]. It means, whatever you lived, you-- you inscribed in the books that you lived there. And I didn't belong there, to [? Pustanitska ?] Street. I was living somewhere in another suburb.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the card.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the card. So this was a big problem. The Germans are coming, two Germans, and they look for the papers. I showed them my [INAUDIBLE]. I was trembling. But they didn't see it, probably. And they said, what you doing here?

speaker: interviewer
text: So luckily, the mother-- who was a seamstress or she-- she was a dressmaker-- she said, this is my helper. She comes here and we had a lot of work and she was sleeping here. Normally, she doesn't live here. And that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they went to the last floor and, in a few days-- in-- no, next day, I heard from the-- from the owners of the flat that they were looking for two Communists. It wasn't about Jews. They were looking for two Communists who were hiding somewhere in the vicinity. And they didn't find anyone. Anyway, it's not important. They decided that they-- I-- I have to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family decided.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at least for a few days. And we had a friend that was my father's friend who was-- of course, he build a house for himself, a one-story house. And from meager sort of [POLISH], savings. And he paid it off. And whatever he owed my father, my father said, forget about it, when the war started. And he said, Mr. Strosberg, I help you whenever you need me, because you are good man. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I recall something which I don't know whether I told you. I was being in the ghetto. And the Polish man who brought the food-- but when he left, he said to the priest-- did I say that? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to the priest, [POLISH] Sztuka-- Sztuka was his name. And he knew my father very well, because my father gave the donation, the [POLISH]. That's the, you know, the noise which marks the-- bell-- the bell, for the bell, the whole building with the bell. My father gave a donation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was very friendly with the sisters, you know, of the order and with that priest. He was friendly with everyone. And the priest, on Sunday during the sermon, he said there was one great man among us. It was Strosberg, and he's not alive anymore. He thought so, because the other man told him that my father is dying of typhoid. This is a digression or whatever you call it. But I was talking about--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about having to leave for a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Having to leave. So that Mr. Kupniewski, he was, sort of, like, in the army. So it's one who is taking the papers, you know, a go-between sort of. He was the one who knew where my parents were, every one of them. He knew where everyone was hidden, but never together. Because my father said, if we are together, we are likely to get killed. And there is a chance, if we are separated, that one or two of us will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought, I have to run away. My mother is not here, I don't know where. I went to the-- sort of-- it was before the war, it was a Jewish farm from the [HEBREW]. They were going-- they-- they had cattle over there and other things, you know, and a farm. And they were going after so-and-so many years, they were going to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there. Not a-- a soul. And I was crying. And I didn't know what to do. And the next night, I decide to go. It was early in the morning. I decided to go to Mr. Kupniewski, who-- before the war-- he was a policeman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a simple man, but a very good man. He was coming to me and coming to my parents. You know? And I'm going there, but very carefully. Because people can recognize me, anyone. You know? And there is a chance that they go to the police and announce me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was already in the house, or the backyard. And every backyard was a altar with a [POLISH] Maria. And every night, the Poles used to get together and pray [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- sort of on the backyard. And I came to the backyard. I didn't know which floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, some little boys, about 10, 12-- they were the worst-- were following me. And I hide behind the Maria [POLISH]. You know? And I had-- I was very lucky then. Because that-- that policeman had a son. About-- He could've been about 16 or 18. I don't remember. And they called him and said, you know, a Jewess is hiding here. And he-- and he knew me. You know? He saw me once or so. And they found me. And you go-- we're going quick to the police to say that a Jewess--! And like, you know, like it was a monster. And I thought, it's my end.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. I'm not hiding. I'm looking for a-- for a toilet, I said. And he said, all right. I show you where is the toilet. And he talked to the boys. And he said, you go outside and wait for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, he took me on the first floor to his parents' place. It was a small flat, and they hide me in the wardrobe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they said, you can't stay here. Because they-- they will make a big noise about you. Because they were outside, and they didn't see you coming out. And really, the same night, they were looking themself-- from the roof to the bottom-- they were looking for the victim. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr. Kupniewski said, don't get nervous. I-- I will somehow arrange it. And you go to your-- I take you to your place where you were before. And that's how it was. And the next night, the SS was there, looking everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd already gone by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was gone, yes. So that was another miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came back, and I said, Mrs. Yrene-- Irena-- I won't be long here. I'm waiting for my mother, and she will take me somewhere. And that Kupniewski already let my mother knew-- know about it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, so, they said, all right, but as quick as possible. And then, my mother was really in big trouble. Because she didn't know anyone anymore. And she met a Jewish man who was in the same trade with my father. And she said to him, we know-- you know I've got a daughter. And-- and I haven't got a place for her to go. And he said, I don't know. He had a hiding place. But he was-- you know, it was his friend and he couldn't help it. But my mother said, please, think about something. You've got-- you know many people. Simon's my husband and might be someone. And he said, wait a minute. There is one man, and maybe he'll-- he'll be able to help you-- another policeman. And so, my mother took the address and went there. She went there. And they say, no, of course not. We can't-- we can't help you, not at all. So my mother said, maybe you know someone who can. And they said, no. And-- but have a cup of tea. They were quite friendly, because they knew who my mother was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, they-- giving her a cup of tea. She's drinking it. And she's-- she's-- that was all in the kitchen. And she notice that the door-- the other door was closed. All right, it was closed. And then, she notice that on the table, there were [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The why the Jews might-- used to make the [INAUDIBLE]. No Pole could do it. Because it was so fine, it was-- it was a special school to make it. You know? And my mother is getting up and says to the woman, I know that you're hiding Jews here, so maybe you could help my daughter too. They knew [LAUGHS] they had to admit. You know? Because my mother wasn't not so easily-- they wouldn't give rid of my mother. You know? And of course, not straight away, but they-- my mother said how she knows. And they said, we've got a cousin-- distant cousin who lives in [? Radocz. ?] That was [? Linia ?] [? Otwalska. ?] That was the place where the-- many people were going for holidays, you know, fresh air, not far from Warsaw. It was a-- a little sort of train going and, you know, very noisy, and everyone knew that line. And-- but [? Radocz ?] was especially Polish place. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, I've got a-- the family, they've got one daughter. And maybe-- it's a-- you know, they are very poor. They used to be-- he used to be-- he used to work in a office. But they're class people. And maybe-- I don't promise anything. And they made a meeting. And the woman said, I have to agree, because I'm already keeping a Jewish boy. The boy was seven or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took him, because they were poor. And the parents were from another town. The-- the mother was-- the father was a solicitor and mother was also professional-- I can't recall. They both-- the father, when he came last time-- what was Mrs. Poniewierski who was hiding us. She was telling me afterwards, she could see that he's a goner. You know? It was-- The father-- that the father was?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't-- he didn't look Jewish, but he was a goner. He resigned. You know? It was-- he didn't have the power to fight. He lost it. And she knew it. But it was the mother who was coming also. And the mother apparently wanted to go to Germany, because she was looking Jewish. You know? And they caught her and killed her on the spot. She was already on the [? [INAUDIBLE] ?] side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the boy left, to whom they were very, very attached. And they wanted to keep the-- the child. And they-- so therefore-- and they had a problem-child with the daughter, and they saw that I would be a good influence. Also, she was playing piano. I was playing piano. You see, we had many things in common.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was, you know, over-sexed. She was a [POLISH]. If I tell you about her-- once she brought a German to the house. And she said, Tola, [INAUDIBLE], you have to hide. Because, you know, I've got a new boyfriend. Every second-- she found a Jew, a dentist, who had a wife and little children. She had sex with him. She brought once his son. She was incredible. She was just incredible. She was sick, over-sexed. And they wanted me to have a good sort of rapport with her, and maybe she will change. She didn't change. But I was her confidant. She used to tell me everything. And she loved me like her own sister. She had very old parents. They had her-- the mother had her, she was in her 40s. She was a intelligent woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, it was a old babushka there, might be 90. I don't know. And she was cooking. She was cooking awful. You know? And-- and the mother wasn't a housewife. She was trying to get some money. You know, she was selling bread. And when I came, already, financially they were better off, because my mother used to pay for me and for the boy. And they lived from it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, one day, I said, you know, I can cook. Because in the old place, when they went for-- I used to watch how they cook. But when they went to the church, they said, you know, mix, but don't touch. And I was so hungry. I wouldn't try a spoon of soup. You know, because it was taboo. It wasn't mine. You know, so I was mixing and, you know, not trying.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they but they taught you, then, how to cook, by you watching.

speaker: interviewee
text: By my watching. So I-- I thought I'd tell them. Because the-- the-- the soups and everything was outrageous. And I thought that I will have a better rapport with them if I cook. And they said, yes, we would like you to. But what about the grandmother? The grandmother, all the time, she was praying and she was, you know, and-- crossing herself. And she didn't talk. She was very old. But the-- Mrs. Poniewierskis's sister used to come and brother. And they knew me. Over there, I was a different person. Because it was fresh air. It was-- sort of the houses weren't so close. The house wasn't finished. But we were sort of living on a terrace, which was me and the-- Krystyna was her name. You know. And we were talking all night. She was telling me all her stories. One--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go outside? Could you leave the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once or twice, her aunt took me. It was a-- a forest, growing forest. It was so high. And we used to hide. And she used to tell me that she loves me very much. She was an old maid. She have someone, nothing came out. And she's looking at me as I am a saint. And I thought, I'm a saint, because I'm forced to. [LAUGHS] But actually, don't know who I am. But I like them very much. And they all look Jewish, you know, which was another thing. I thought that I was hiding. But it was-- it was just-- I don't know-- might be generations there was some Jew. But her brother was a typical Jew, you know, from looks.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they all like me, because I was pleasant. And I was trying to be nice. I wanted to be there. You know. That was the first place when I felt a bit human. You know? And I had a argument with this-- the boy. And I remember that-- With the boy that-- the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one that was being hidden with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because the boy was awfully shielded there. He could do-- it was said that those are aunt and uncle, and he's one of the family. And the next door were some-- not Germans, but sort of neo-Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] --

speaker: interviewee
text: The Volksdeutsches. Volksdeutsches. And I don't know if they knew or they didn't know. Probably, they didn't know, because they all looked Jewish. You know, and it's somehow-- but the boy was very noisy. And once, I said, [? Taldik, ?] if you'll be so noisy, I will hit you. You know, because don't forget, that Germans can come and-- and fetch us. And he was crying. And he went to his aunt and he said, I'm leaving that place. I'm going to the bush, you know, because she told me off. You know, he was spoiled. But they had that against me. But I was-- I was full of fear. I was afraid. But Krystyna, the daughter, was my best friend at the time. Once, she told me, look, I won't come for two days. She had an appointment with her boyfriend. And she said, don't-- I'm-- I'm putting the papers-- the school papers-- under the bed. And you find it. And you say that, probably, I was arrested. And it was so hard for me. She was with some boyfriend. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But she wanted that as an excuse or as a story?

speaker: interviewee
text: An excuse. She didn't want to break their heart in one way. But she broke their heart in the other way. And the whole place-- they were nice families, sort of middle class. And nobody talked to her, because she was a outrageous person, you know, in those times. So-- but-- I was the only friend she had. Let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, just don't play with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. So anyway, I was-- to the end of the war, I was there. And a bit longer too. And-- because in the meantime, that Krystyna married [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] married a-- a head of police in [? Rasocz. ?] He was a solicitor from profession. He was a head of police in [? Radocz. ?] And it was a wedding. And it was said-- I had a whole story, that my parents had-- because it was after the uprising in Warsaw-- that my parents had a shop and probably were killed. And I-- so I had to stay. I came for the wedding. I have to stay here. And that was accepted. But when Praga was already free, you know, it was in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberated. First steps-- I knew only where my brother is. My brother didn't know where I am. So of course, I ran to the place to-- to find out whether my brother is alive. That was at Mr. Freund. I told you before. I mentioned him. He was a very decent man, but he was old. And he was-- my brother was hiding there. And the wife was Polish. And they couldn't have children. So it was so-called that they-- they adopted my brother. But officially, he couldn't be. He had-- had to fight-- hide. And Mr. Freund used to come and read him-- give him papers and all sorts of books. And he was reading a lot. But the wife didn't give him anything to eat. She wanted-- she didn't have the heart to go to the Germans and say that he's Jewish. But on the other hand, she wanted him to die-- naturally die. And he was looking terrible. When I saw him, I couldn't believe it's my-- I didn't recognize him. It was a skeleton, a walking skeleton. What happened-- and Mr. Freund was a very nice man. He had our two fur coats, which he gave us right away when I came. But he said, you know, [? Yrena, ?] you have to take your brother, because-- I said, but it's-- it's-- we're all-- already liberated. You don't know what's going on here, they said. You see, what happened-- my brother felt that it's-- that the Russians or whoever come here to liberate them, but they didn't tell him. Because they didn't want the neighborhood to know that they hided a Jewish boy. That was the main reason. So my-- my brother was still hidden and treated the same way. When-- you know, when the Russians were bombarding, he was on this-- not in the cellar, he was on the top. And he was so silly as me, making [POLISH], ham, and [POLISH], and other things. Because they have a pig-sty over there or whatever. He never tried anything. And he said he had rotten bread to eat. And he said that he wasn't hungry. And he was happy, because he wasn't hungry. He knew that he will die. So when-- but one thing, he was more free to go to the window. And through the curtains, some people saw him. And they called the Russians, mind you, because the Germans were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans-- the Russians came and said, you're hiding a German here. Would you believe it? And of course, my brother came out, and he said, no. I'm Jewish. But they didn't believe him. I'm Jewish. No, no. He had to put down his pants. And after many talks, they let him be. You know? And so, Mr. Freund, who was a lovely man, he gave me the furs. He-- he was a rich man. He didn't need it. Before we finish the story, we-- we'll have to finish this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll go on with the story in the next tape.
